record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk and Recorder (county-level from formation; state registration 1943)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1907 (births and deaths; compliance uneven in early years, especially rural counties)

burned_county_authority: Montana has no officially designated 'burned counties.' Some frontier-era records are sparse due to late settlement, minimal early government infrastructure, and the transient nature of mining camp populations. The Montana Historical Society (MHS) in Helena is the primary backup repository for county records.
administrative_notes: Montana Territory was organized in 1864 from parts of Idaho, Dakota, and Nebraska territories. Montana was admitted as the 41st state on November 8, 1889. The original nine territorial counties were Beaverhead, Chouteau, Custer (originally Big Horn), Deer Lodge, Edgerton (renamed Lewis and Clark), Gallatin, Jefferson, Madison, and Missoula. Montana has 56 counties, including two consolidated city-counties: Anaconda-Deer Lodge County (consolidated 1977) and Butte-Silver Bow County (consolidated 1977). Each is treated as a single jurisdiction. The state capital has always been Helena (territorial capital moved from Bannack to Virginia City in 1865, then to Helena in 1875). County Clerks and Recorders maintain deed, land, and marriage records. Clerks of District Court handle probate and civil/criminal records. The Montana Historical Society in Helena is the central repository for state and territorial government records, microfilmed county records, mining company records, and historical manuscripts.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Montana has no burned counties. While some territorial-era and frontier records are sparse due to transient mining camp populations and minimal early government infrastructure, this reflects settlement conditions rather than catastrophic record loss. County records generally survive from the date of county organization. The Montana Historical Society holds microfilm copies of many county records as a preservation backup.

core_repositories.description: Central state repository for Montana records. Houses territorial and state government records, governor's papers, legislative records, mining company records (including Anaconda Copper Mining Company), microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, probate), livestock brand books, prison records (1871+), WPA records, extensive photographic collections, and the Montana state vital records index. The Research Center is the primary starting point for Montana genealogical research.

Statewide genealogical society that publishes Montana surname indexes, county research guides, and the journal Treasure State Lines. Maintains a research library and coordinates with local county genealogy societies across Montana.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Montana — Mansfield Library Archives
core_repositories.description: Archives and Special Collections holds extensive Montana manuscript collections including lumber industry records, Forest Service historical files, Anaconda Copper Mining Company records, labor union records, and family papers. The Northwest Montana historical photograph collection is particularly strong.

Holds federal records for Montana including General Land Office (GLO) homestead and preemption entries, Bureau of Indian Affairs records for all seven Montana reservations (Blackfeet, Crow, Flathead, Fort Belknap, Fort Peck, Northern Cheyenne, Rocky Boy), federal court records (including naturalization), Bureau of Reclamation irrigation project records, and military post records for Forts Benton, Keogh, Assinniboine, Shaw, Missoula, and others.

familysearch_collections.description: Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, probate, court records, and vital records. Many browse-only image collections from Montana county clerks. Also includes indexed collections of Montana vital records, cemetery records, tax rolls, and naturalization records.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability charts, and links to digitized microfilm collections for Montana.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Montana newspapers including territorial-era papers, mining camp newspapers, and county weeklies. Coverage from the 1860s forward. Montana Newspapers (montananewspapers.org) provides additional digitized content.

MHS holds the largest collection of Montana newspapers on microfilm. Includes territorial-era papers from mining camps, county weeklies, and ethnic community newspapers. Interlibrary loan available for many titles.

military_records.description: Records from Montana military forts including Fort Benton, Fort Shaw, Fort Ellis, Fort Keogh (Miles City), Fort Assinniboine (Havre), Fort Missoula, Fort Logan, and Fort Custer. Post returns, muster rolls, hospital records, and correspondence document soldiers and their families.

Montana Historical Society holds veterans' service records, discharge papers (DD-214) filed with county clerks, and veterans' grave registrations. WPA veterans' graves survey from the 1930s-40s covers many counties. State soldiers' home records also at MHS.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Montana including homestead, preemption, desert land, and timber culture entries. Searchable database covers all federal land transactions. Montana was almost entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership. The homestead boom (1909-1918) generated hundreds of thousands of entries.
County Clerks and Recorders maintain grantor/grantee deed indexes and deed books from county formation. In-person access at each county clerk's office. Some counties have digitized indexes.

Livestock brand registrations from territorial era forward. Brand books identify ranchers by name, county, and brand design. Essential for tracing ranching families. Historical brand books at MHS; current records at the Montana Department of Livestock.

vital_records.description: State registration of births and deaths from 1907. Marriage records at state level from 1943 (earlier at county). Certified copies available by mail or in person. Some early records have gaps in rural and frontier counties.

County clerks recorded births, deaths, and marriages before state registration. Coverage varies by county — some have records from territorial formation. Marriage licenses at county level are often the earliest vital records available.

church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery transcriptions compiled by DAR chapters, county historical societies, and WPA workers. MHS holds WPA cemetery survey records. FindAGrave has extensive Montana coverage. USGenWeb Tombstone Project includes some Montana counties.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Catholic Diocese and Mission Records — Montana
church_and_cemetery.repository: Diocese of Helena / Diocese of Great Falls-Billings / Gonzaga University
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic church records are among Montana's most important genealogical sources. Jesuit mission records (St. Mary's 1841, St. Ignatius 1854, St. Peter's 1859) predate territorial government. Mining town parishes in Butte, Anaconda, and Helena served Irish, Italian, Croatian, and other Catholic immigrant communities. Oregon Province Jesuit Archives at Gonzaga University in Spokane hold mission records. Many parish registers are on FamilySearch.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Free genealogy resources organized by county. Includes transcribed records, cemetery readings, family histories, and links to county-specific resources contributed by volunteers.

Exceptional local archives holding Butte city directories (1884+), mine employment records, ethnic church records, Sanborn fire insurance maps, naturalization records, and Anaconda Copper Mining Company records. Essential for Butte and Silver Bow County genealogy.

historical_counties_extinct.repository: Montana Historical Society / Lewis and Clark County Clerk
historical_counties_extinct.description: Edgerton County was one of the original nine Montana Territory counties created in 1865, named for territorial governor Sidney Edgerton. It was renamed Lewis and Clark County in 1868. All Edgerton County records continued under Lewis and Clark County — no records were lost in the renaming. Researchers should search under both names in territorial-era documents at MHS.

historical_counties_extinct.repository: Montana Historical Society / Custer County Clerk
historical_counties_extinct.description: The original Big Horn County was one of the nine original Montana Territory counties in 1865. It was renamed Custer County in 1877 after the Battle of the Little Bighorn. The current Big Horn County was created in 1913 as a separate entity from Rosebud and Yellowstone counties. Researchers looking for pre-1877 'Big Horn County' records should check Custer County files.

historical_counties_extinct.repository: Montana Historical Society / Dawson County Clerk
historical_counties_extinct.description: Dawson County was created in 1869 and reorganized multiple times as parent territory was carved into new counties across eastern Montana. The county's boundaries changed dramatically between 1869 and 1920 as Richland, Wibaux, Prairie, McCone, and other counties were separated. Researchers should trace boundary changes to determine which modern county holds records for a specific location and time period.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Crow Agency Records — Bureau of Indian Affairs
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Crow Indian Reservation (Big Horn County) records include Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records under the Dawes Act, annuity rolls, school records (Crow Agency schools), and agency correspondence. Records at NARA Denver and the BIA Crow Agency in Crow Agency, MT.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Northern Cheyenne — Tongue River Agency Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Tongue River Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Northern Cheyenne Indian Reservation (Rosebud County) records include census rolls, allotment records, school files (Busby and Lame Deer schools), and agency correspondence at NARA Denver. The Northern Cheyenne fought for their own reservation separate from the Crow — tribal records document this distinct history.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Blackfeet Agency, Browning
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Blackfeet Indian Reservation (Glacier County) records include Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school attendance records (Blackfeet Boarding School, Cut Bank Boarding School), and agency correspondence at NARA Denver. The Blackfeet ceded lands that became Glacier National Park in 1895.

Flathead Indian Reservation (Lake, Missoula, Flathead, Sanders counties) is home to the Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes. BIA records include census rolls, allotment records, school files (St. Ignatius Mission School, Ursuline Academy), and agency correspondence at NARA Denver. The Hellgate Treaty of 1855 established the reservation.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Fort Belknap Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Fort Belknap Indian Reservation (Blaine, Phillips counties) is home to the Assiniboine and Gros Ventre (Aaniiih) tribes. BIA records include census rolls, allotment records, school files, and agency correspondence at NARA Denver.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Fort Peck Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Fort Peck Indian Reservation (Roosevelt, Valley, Daniels, Sheridan counties) is home to the Assiniboine and Sioux tribes. BIA records include census rolls, allotment records, school attendance (Poplar and Wolf Point schools), and agency correspondence at NARA Denver.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Rocky Boy Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Rocky Boy's Indian Reservation (Hill, Chouteau counties) is home to the Chippewa-Cree. Established in 1916, it is Montana's newest and smallest reservation. BIA records include census rolls, allotment records, school files, and agency correspondence at NARA Denver. Rocky Boy was carved from the former Fort Assinniboine military reservation.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Denver / County District Courts / MHS
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records filed in Montana district courts from territorial era forward. Many Montana immigrants naturalized locally — court records contain declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization with biographical details. Butte, Anaconda, and Great Falls had especially high naturalization volumes due to mining and smelting immigration. Some naturalization indexes at MHS and NARA Denver. Post-1906 naturalization records at NARA.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Ethnic Community Records — Butte and Mining Towns
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Butte-Silver Bow Public Archives / MHS / University of Montana
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Montana's mining communities attracted Irish, Cornish, Finnish, Serbian, Croatian, Italian, Chinese, and other immigrant groups. Ethnic fraternal organization records (Ancient Order of Hibernians, Finnish temperance societies, Serbian lodges), ethnic church records, and foreign-language newspaper archives document these communities. The Butte-Silver Bow Public Archives has the strongest concentration of ethnic community records.

Montana's homestead boom (1909-1918) brought thousands of Scandinavian, German-Russian, and other immigrant homesteaders, many recruited by railroad colonization departments. Great Northern Railway and Northern Pacific Railway promotional materials and colonist records at the Minnesota Historical Society and MHS identify immigrant settlers and their origins.
